heroOverview: Dorsalgia — the clinical term for pain located anywhere along the spine from the cervical region to the sacrum — is the leading cause of disability worldwide, affecting approximately 619 million people at any one time according to the 2020 Global Burden of Disease study. The great majority of back pain episodes are mechanical in origin, meaning they arise from muscles, ligaments, facet joints, or intervertebral discs rather than from an underlying disease. Roughly 80-85% of adults will experience at least one significant episode of back pain in their lifetime, and up to 23% of adults globally live with chronic low back pain. While most acute episodes resolve within four to six weeks, back pain carries an enormous economic burden through lost productivity and healthcare utilisation. Understanding the distinction between non-specific mechanical back pain and pain with a specific, potentially serious cause is the foundation of appropriate management.

keyStats.prevalence: Affects approximately 619 million people globally at any one point in time (GBD 2020 study); the leading cause of years lived with disability worldwide
keyStats.demographics: Lifetime prevalence approximately 80-85% in adults; highest incidence in adults aged 30-65; affects all demographics but more disabling in low-income settings
keyStats.avgAge: Peak incidence of first episode in adults aged 35-55; chronic back pain most prevalent in people aged 40-80
keyStats.globalCases: About 619 million prevalent cases in 2020; projected to rise to 843 million by 2050 (Lancet Rheumatology 2023)

definition: Dorsalgia (ICD-10: M54.9) is a non-specific diagnostic code denoting pain, aching, or discomfort arising from any part of the spine or its supporting structures — vertebral bodies, intervertebral discs, facet joints, ligaments, paraspinal muscles, and nerve roots — when a more specific diagnosis cannot yet be assigned. The condition is classified under orthopedics and, for complex or chronic cases, also involves rheumatology, neurology, or pain medicine. Clinically, back pain is categorised by duration (acute: less than 6 weeks; subacute: 6-12 weeks; chronic: more than 12 weeks), by region (cervical, thoracic, lumbar, sacral), and by the presence or absence of specific pathology. Non-specific low back pain — pain without an identifiable structural cause on imaging — accounts for approximately 90% of presentations. Specific causes include disc herniation, spinal stenosis, vertebral fracture, spondylolisthesis, infection, malignancy, and inflammatory arthropathies such as ankylosing spondylitis. Neurogenic pain (radiculopathy), where a compressed nerve root produces pain, numbness, or weakness in a limb, requires a different treatment approach from purely axial pain. The relationship between imaging findings and symptoms is imperfect: disc bulges, facet arthropathy, and degenerative changes are present in large proportions of asymptomatic adults.

typesClassification.description: Pain in the lumbar and sacral region without a specific identifiable structural cause on clinical assessment or imaging; accounts for approximately 90% of back pain presentations and usually responds to conservative management.
Pain, numbness, tingling, or weakness radiating into the arm or leg along a dermatomal pattern, caused by compression or irritation of a spinal nerve root, most often by disc herniation or stenosis.
Pain localised to the neck and upper back, often involving cervical disc disease, facet joint arthropathy, or muscle tension, and sometimes accompanied by headache or arm symptoms.

Pain in the mid-back region between the neck and lumbar spine; less common than lumbar pain, but important because thoracic back pain is more frequently associated with specific pathology including vertebral fracture, malignancy, or aortic disease.

Persistent pain lasting more than 12 weeks as a primary diagnosis, where central sensitisation and psychosocial factors play a major role; managed with a biopsychosocial approach including rehabilitation, cognitive behavioural therapy, and pain medicine.

primarySymptoms: Aching or stiffness along the spine that may be localised to one region or diffuse, typically worse in the morning and after prolonged sitting or standing.
Sharp pain with specific movements such as bending, lifting, twisting, or transitioning from sitting to standing, reflecting a mechanical component.
Muscle spasm or a feeling of tightness in the paraspinal muscles flanking the spine, which the body uses as a protective splinting mechanism around an injured area.
Pain that radiates from the back into the buttocks or posterior thigh (referred pain), which may occur with lumbar disc disease or sacroiliac joint dysfunction without true nerve root compression.
Shooting, burning, or electric-shock pain travelling from the lower back down one leg past the knee into the calf or foot (sciatica), indicating actual nerve root involvement.
Numbness or pins-and-needles in a leg, foot, arm, or hand following a dermatomal pattern, suggesting compression of a specific nerve root.
Weakness in a limb — such as foot drop, difficulty rising on tiptoes, or grip weakness — indicating significant nerve root or spinal cord compromise.
Pain that eases when lying flat and worsens with prolonged sitting or standing, characteristic of mechanical or discogenic pain.
Neck pain with restricted rotation or lateral flexion, headaches at the base of the skull, and occasional dizziness when cervical structures are involved.
A feeling of instability or giving way in the spine, particularly with movement, that may suggest spondylolisthesis or significant disc degeneration.
earlyWarningSigns: Gradually worsening back stiffness in the morning that takes more than 30 minutes to ease — an early feature of inflammatory back disease.
Pain beginning after an identifiable triggering event such as heavy lifting, an awkward twist, or prolonged driving.
Mild muscle tightness or tenderness to palpation along the spine that does not settle with a day's rest.
Recurrent episodes of sudden back 'locking' with muscle spasm that resolve spontaneously.
emergencySigns: Saddle anaesthesia — numbness or tingling in the groin, inner thighs, and perineum — or loss of bowel and bladder control, which indicate cauda equina syndrome, a surgical emergency requiring immediate hospital assessment.
Rapidly progressive weakness in both legs, suggesting spinal cord compression that requires urgent imaging and intervention.
Severe constant back pain that is unrelated to movement, worse at night, accompanied by unexplained weight loss or fever, which raises concern for malignancy or spinal infection.
Back pain following significant trauma such as a fall from height, road traffic accident, or direct spinal impact — risk of vertebral fracture.
New back pain in someone with known cancer or osteoporosis, which may indicate a pathological fracture or metastasis.

causes.description: The nucleus pulposus, the gel-like centre of an intervertebral disc, herniates through the outer annulus fibrosus under compressive load, pressing against an adjacent nerve root; this triggers both mechanical compression and a local inflammatory cascade involving phospholipase A2 and cytokines that sensitise the nerve and produce radicular pain, numbness, and weakness in the corresponding dermatomal distribution.

The paired facet (zygapophysial) joints carry approximately 20% of the axial load in the lumbar spine; repeated stress, ageing, and loss of disc height lead to cartilage degradation, subchondral sclerosis, and osteophyte formation similar to osteoarthritis, producing localised pain that characteristically worsens with spinal extension and rotation.

causes.cause: Paraspinal muscle strain and ligamentous injury
causes.description: Sudden overloading of the erector spinae, multifidus, and supporting ligaments — from heavy lifting with poor posture, a twisting injury, or a fall — produces microtears and localised inflammation; pain is typically acute, reproducible on specific movements, and resolves within two to four weeks with activity.

Progressive narrowing of the spinal canal or the lateral foramina through which nerve roots exit — caused by disc bulging, facet hypertrophy, and ligamentum flavum thickening accumulating over decades — compresses the spinal cord or nerve roots; the hallmark symptom is neurogenic claudication, pain or weakness in the legs that worsens with walking and standing but eases with sitting or lumbar flexion.

Ankylosing spondylitis and related spondyloarthropathies cause inflammation at the sacroiliac joints and vertebral endplates; the pain characteristically begins in young adults under 40, improves with exercise rather than rest, is associated with morning stiffness lasting more than 30 minutes, and over years can lead to fusion of the spine.

In individuals with reduced bone mineral density — postmenopausal women, older men, and people on long-term corticosteroids — minor forces can fracture a vertebral body; the fracture causes acute, localised, activity-related pain, and multiple compression fractures progressively reduce height and produce kyphosis.

Chronic back pain involves significant amplification by the central nervous system: psychological factors including depression, anxiety, catastrophising, fear-avoidance behaviour, and low self-efficacy maintain and intensify pain independently of ongoing tissue damage; these factors are among the strongest predictors of transition from acute to chronic pain.

riskFactors.description: Jobs requiring repetitive heavy lifting, awkward postures, whole-body vibration (such as driving), and prolonged standing carry a two- to fourfold increased risk of low back pain; ergonomic interventions and workload management reduce incidence.

Extended sitting, especially with poor lumbar support, increases disc pressure and weakens core muscles that stabilise the spine; people who sit more than 8 hours per day have higher rates of back pain, and regular movement breaks reduce risk.

Excess body weight increases compressive loads on lumbar discs and facet joints; each unit increase in BMI above 25 is associated with a modestly increased risk of back pain, and weight loss reliably reduces pain severity in overweight individuals.

Depression and anxiety are among the strongest individual predictors of the transition from acute to chronic back pain; psychological factors drive central sensitisation, increase pain catastrophising, and reduce engagement with rehabilitation.

Intervertebral discs progressively lose water content, height, and elasticity with age from the third decade onward; degenerative changes are near-universal by age 60, though their clinical significance varies widely.
A prior episode of low back pain is the single strongest predictor of recurrence; approximately 40-60% of people who recover from acute back pain will have at least one recurrence within a year.

Smoking reduces disc nutrition by impairing small vessel blood flow to vertebral endplates, accelerates disc degeneration, and impairs bone mineral density; smokers have a significantly higher rate of disc disease and chronic back pain than non-smokers.

Twin studies attribute 30-60% of lumbar disc degeneration variance to genetic factors; specific polymorphisms affecting collagen, aggrecan, and matrix metalloproteinase genes influence disc integrity, but clinical implications of genetic testing are not yet established.

affectedDemographics: Adults aged 30-65, who bear the highest burden through occupational exposure and disc degeneration
Manual workers in construction, nursing, and freight handling, with high physical spinal loading
Office workers and drivers with prolonged sitting and sedentary jobs
Postmenopausal women with osteoporosis, at risk of vertebral compression fractures
Young adults aged 18-40 with inflammatory spondyloarthropathy, where back pain is often misattributed for years
Athletes in high-load or repetitive spinal sports such as rowing, gymnastics, weightlifting, and cricket
Older adults with spinal stenosis and neurogenic claudication
People with depression, anxiety, or chronic widespread pain, who have higher rates of chronic back pain
diagnosisOverview: Assessment of back pain begins with distinguishing the large majority of benign mechanical presentations from the small minority with specific or serious pathology. Clinicians use 'red flag' features to identify conditions requiring urgent investigation: saddle anaesthesia or sphincter disturbance (cauda equina syndrome), progressive neurological deficit, constant non-mechanical pain, fever, unexplained weight loss, night sweats, a history of cancer, age over 50 with a new presentation, and recent significant trauma. In the absence of red flags, imaging is not recommended within the first four to six weeks in most guidelines, because early MRI or CT rarely changes management for acute mechanical pain and may lead to unnecessary intervention. When imaging is indicated — persistent pain beyond six weeks, neurological deficit, or red flags — MRI is the preferred modality because it shows disc, nerve, and soft tissue anatomy without radiation. Plain radiographs are used when fracture or alignment is in question. CT myelography is reserved for patients unable to undergo MRI. Blood tests including ESR, CRP, full blood count, alkaline phosphatase, and protein electrophoresis help when malignancy, infection, or inflammatory disease is suspected. Formal neurological examination with dermatomal sensory mapping, myotomal strength testing, and reflex assessment identifies the level and severity of nerve root compression. The straight leg raise test has reasonable sensitivity for L4-S5 disc herniation. Functional assessment tools and validated pain questionnaires (Oswestry Disability Index, Roland-Morris) help quantify impairment and track progress.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identify red flags, characterise pain type (mechanical vs. inflammatory vs. neurogenic), and determine whether immediate investigation or referral is needed before imaging is ordered.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician asks detailed questions about pain onset, character, aggravating and relieving factors, and associated neurological symptoms, followed by examination of spine mobility, reflexes, strength, and sensation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The gold-standard investigation for disc herniation, spinal stenosis, nerve root compression, infection, and malignancy; provides anatomical detail of disc, nerves, bone marrow, and soft tissues without radiation.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You lie still inside a large cylindrical magnet for 30-45 minutes; it is noisy but painless. Contrast dye may be injected into a vein if infection or tumour is suspected.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses vertebral alignment, disc space height, and bone density; first-line for suspected fracture, spondylolisthesis, or severe degeneration, particularly in older patients.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Quick standing or lying X-rays; results are immediate. Multiple views (anteroposterior and lateral) are taken to cover the region of interest.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Provides superior bony detail compared to MRI for complex fractures, ossification of ligaments, or in patients who cannot undergo MRI; useful for surgical planning.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A few minutes lying in a ring-shaped scanner; involves ionising radiation. Contrast may be used for vascular or infectious pathology.

diagnosticTests.test: Blood tests (ESR, CRP, CBC, protein electrophoresis)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Screen for inflammatory spondyloarthropathy, infection, and haematological malignancy such as multiple myeloma when clinical features suggest a systemic cause.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw from an arm vein; results return within hours for inflammatory markers, and overnight for protein electrophoresis.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measure bone density to diagnose or grade osteoporosis in patients at risk of vertebral fragility fracture, guiding treatment with bisphosphonates or other bone-protective agents.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A low-radiation X-ray scan of the lumbar spine and hip taking 10-15 minutes; painless and requires no special preparation.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of dorsalgia is guided by pain duration, the specific cause, and the degree of functional impairment. For acute non-specific back pain, the most strongly evidence-based first-line intervention is staying active — bed rest beyond two days is associated with poorer outcomes. NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen) provide modest but consistent short-term pain relief and are first-choice pharmacological therapy. Muscle relaxants are sometimes added for severe spasm but carry sedative risk. WHO and NICE guidelines caution against opioid use for non-specific back pain given the risk of dependence and their limited long-term effectiveness. Exercise therapy — particularly strengthening of the lumbar stabilisers and posterior chain — has the strongest evidence base for both acute management and prevention of recurrence. Physiotherapy guided by a musculoskeletal specialist is recommended for people who do not recover within 2-4 weeks. For radicular pain from disc herniation, most cases resolve conservatively within 6-12 weeks; epidural steroid injections can provide short-term relief to enable function while awaiting natural resolution. Surgery is considered for disc herniation only when radiculopathy persists beyond 6-12 weeks despite adequate conservative treatment or when there is progressive neurological deficit. Spinal stenosis with significant claudication or neurological deficit may require decompressive laminectomy or, in selected patients, spinal fusion. Chronic back pain management requires a biopsychosocial model: a combination of supervised exercise, psychological interventions (cognitive behavioural therapy, acceptance and commitment therapy), pain education, and judicious use of adjuvant analgesics such as duloxetine or low-dose tricyclics. Spinal cord stimulation is a fourth-line option for refractory neuropathic pain.

medicalTreatments.name: NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen, diclofenac)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line pharmacological therapy for acute and subacute back pain; reduce prostaglandin-mediated inflammation at disc and facet joint levels. Use at the lowest effective dose for the shortest period to minimise gastrointestinal and cardiovascular risk; gastric protection (PPI) is advised for prolonged use.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Systematic reviews show NSAIDs provide approximately 10-15 points reduction on a 100-point pain scale versus placebo for acute low back pain — modest but clinically meaningful in the short term.

medicalTreatments.name: Supervised exercise therapy
medicalTreatments.description: Structured rehabilitation focusing on lumbar stabilisation, core strengthening, flexibility, and aerobic conditioning; delivered by a physiotherapist. The most effective intervention for preventing recurrence and improving function in chronic low back pain. Programmes of 8-12 supervised sessions over 6-8 weeks show the best outcomes.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Exercise therapy reduces pain and disability by approximately 10-15 points versus inactive controls in chronic back pain (Cochrane review 2017); effect on recurrence prevention is approximately 30-40% relative reduction.

medicalTreatments.name: Duloxetine
medicalTreatments.description: A serotonin-noradrenaline reuptake inhibitor (SNRI) approved for chronic musculoskeletal pain; modulates descending pain inhibitory pathways. Useful when centralised pain or comorbid depression contributes to chronic back pain.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: A 2021 Cochrane review found duloxetine produced clinically significant pain reduction (>30% decrease) in approximately 40% more patients than placebo for chronic low back pain.

medicalTreatments.name: Epidural corticosteroid injection
medicalTreatments.description: Corticosteroid (usually triamcinolone or methylprednisolone) plus local anaesthetic injected into the epidural space under image guidance; reduces periradicular inflammation and oedema around a compressed nerve root, providing a window for rehabilitation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Produces approximately 50% short-term pain relief in 40-70% of patients with acute disc herniation and radiculopathy; effect wanes by 3-6 months and does not alter the long-term natural history.

medicalTreatments.name: Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) for pain
medicalTreatments.description: A structured psychological intervention targeting maladaptive pain-related cognitions, fear-avoidance beliefs, and catastrophising; delivered by a psychologist or trained physiotherapist, typically in 8-12 sessions. Most effective when combined with exercise in chronic pain programmes.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: CBT reduces chronic back pain intensity by 15-30% and disability by 20-40% versus usual care in multiple RCTs; effects are durable at 12-month follow-up in most trials.

surgicalOptions.name: Microdiscectomy
surgicalOptions.description: Minimally invasive removal of the herniated disc fragment compressing a nerve root; performed under general anaesthesia through a small posterior incision with microscope assistance. Indicated for lumbar disc herniation with radiculopathy persisting beyond 6-12 weeks of conservative management, or with progressive neurological deficit.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Approximately 80-90% of patients report significant leg pain relief post-operatively; back pain outcomes are more variable. Recurrence rate of 5-10% within 10 years.

surgicalOptions.name: Decompressive laminectomy
surgicalOptions.description: Removal of bone and thickened ligamentum flavum from the posterior spinal canal to relieve neural compression in lumbar spinal stenosis; indicated for neurogenic claudication causing significant walking limitation or neurological deficit unresponsive to conservative management.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Approximately 60-80% of carefully selected patients achieve significant functional improvement; results are best in those with confirmed stenosis on imaging and classic neurogenic claudication.

surgicalOptions.name: Spinal fusion
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical stabilisation of one or more spinal segments using bone graft and metalwork to eliminate motion at degenerate or unstable levels; indicated for spondylolisthesis, segmental instability, or recurrent disc disease at the same level, and sometimes as an adjunct to decompression.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Success rates of approximately 60-70% for carefully selected patients with single-level instability; outcomes are less predictable than discectomy, and adjacent segment disease is a long-term consideration.

alternativeTreatments.name: Spinal manipulation (chiropractic or osteopathic)
alternativeTreatments.description: High-velocity, low-amplitude thrust techniques applied to spinal joints; systematic reviews show modest short-term benefit for acute low back pain comparable to exercise and pharmacotherapy, with a generally good safety profile in the absence of contraindications.

alternativeTreatments.name: Acupuncture
alternativeTreatments.description: NICE guidelines include acupuncture as an option for chronic primary low back pain when other management strategies have failed; evidence supports modest short-term pain reduction, though effect size is small and long-term durability is uncertain.

alternativeTreatments.name: TENS (transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation)
alternativeTreatments.description: Low-voltage electrical stimulation applied to skin over the painful region; evidence for chronic back pain is inconsistent, but TENS is low-risk and some patients find meaningful symptom relief.

whySeeSpecialist: A primary care clinician manages most acute episodes of back pain effectively. Orthopaedic or spinal specialist involvement is indicated when there are red flag features, progressive neurological deficit, diagnostic uncertainty, failure to improve after 6-12 weeks of conservative management, or when surgical consideration is warranted. Rheumatology input is needed when inflammatory spondyloarthropathy is suspected. A pain medicine specialist or multidisciplinary pain programme is appropriate for chronic, complex, or treatment-refractory back pain.
doctorSelectionGuide: For back pain requiring specialist assessment, look for an orthopaedic spine surgeon, neurosurgeon with a spine focus, or a spinal physiotherapy service with access to MRI. For chronic pain, a pain medicine consultant with access to psychological support and multidisciplinary rehabilitation delivers the best long-term outcomes. If inflammatory disease is suspected, a rheumatologist with experience in spondyloarthropathy is the right specialist.
preventionStrategies: Engage in regular strengthening exercise targeting the lumbar stabilisers, core, and posterior chain — even two to three sessions per week significantly reduces recurrence risk.
Maintain a healthy body weight to reduce compressive loading on lumbar discs and facet joints.
Optimise workstation ergonomics: adjust chair height so the lumbar curve is supported, screen at eye level, and take a 2-5 minute movement break every 30-45 minutes of sitting.
Use correct manual handling technique — bend the knees, keep the load close to the body, and avoid simultaneous twisting and lifting.
Quit smoking, which accelerates disc degeneration and impairs bone mineral density.
Address psychological risk factors early: anxiety, depression, and high job dissatisfaction increase the risk of acute back pain becoming chronic.
lifestyleModifications: Stay as active as possible during an acute episode; short walks, gentle stretching, and light daily activities promote healing better than bed rest.
Apply heat (a warm pack) to the affected area for 15-20 minutes several times daily for muscle spasm; ice in the first 48-72 hours can help with acute injury-related inflammation.
Sleep on a medium-firm mattress; a mattress that is too soft reduces lumbar support and worsens pain in most patients.
Practice mindfulness or relaxation techniques to reduce the stress and tension that amplify pain perception.
Break up prolonged sitting with regular standing and movement; a standing desk or adjustable workstation helps if desk work is required for long periods.

recommended: Anti-inflammatory foods including oily fish (omega-3 fatty acids), fruits, vegetables, and whole grains
Adequate calcium and vitamin D from dairy or fortified foods and moderate sun exposure to support bone health
Sufficient protein to support muscle repair and core muscle building
avoid: Excess processed foods and refined sugars, which promote systemic inflammation
Heavy alcohol use, which increases osteoporosis risk and impairs pain modulation
Excessive caffeine if it disrupts sleep quality, which has a significant impact on pain perception

exerciseGuidelines: Exercise is the single most evidence-based intervention for both prevention and treatment of back pain. During an acute episode, continue gentle walking and activity as tolerated; structured rest is not beneficial beyond two days. As pain eases over the first two to four weeks, progress to a supervised programme of lumbar stabilisation, McKenzie extension exercises, swimming, or cycling — all with low spinal compressive load. For chronic back pain, resistance training of the posterior chain combined with aerobic exercise (walking, cycling, aquatic exercise) for a minimum of 150 minutes per week is recommended by most guidelines. Avoid high-impact activities and heavy axial loading until assessed by a physiotherapist if there is active nerve root compression.
dailyManagement: Take prescribed NSAIDs with food at regular intervals rather than waiting for pain to peak for better analgesic effect.
Track pain, activity, and function with a simple daily scale to identify triggers, improvements, and plateau so you can communicate changes to your clinician.
Pace activities — alternate demanding tasks with rest rather than over-exerting until pain flares, then resting completely.
Apply the principles learned in physiotherapy or exercise rehabilitation every day, not just on session days.
Seek review if you develop new numbness, bladder or bowel changes, or if pain is worsening rather than improving on treatment.
prognosis: Most acute episodes of non-specific back pain resolve within four to six weeks regardless of treatment. Roughly 90% of patients with acute low back pain improve significantly within three months, though a substantial proportion have residual symptoms. The critical challenge is prevention of chronicity: approximately 10-15% of acute presentations develop into chronic back pain lasting more than 12 weeks, and these account for the majority of the disability and economic burden attributable to back pain. Psychosocial yellow flags — depression, anxiety, fear-avoidance, low job satisfaction, and catastrophising — are the strongest predictors of poor outcome and should be identified and addressed early. For disc herniation with radiculopathy, 80-90% of patients improve substantially within six weeks with conservative management; surgery accelerates early recovery but produces equivalent long-term outcomes at one year. Spinal stenosis tends to be progressive but slowly; about half of patients remain stable over years without surgery.
recoveryTimeline: Acute non-specific back pain typically improves substantially within two to four weeks and resolves in four to six weeks. Disc herniation with radiculopathy usually improves over six to twelve weeks. Following microdiscectomy, most patients return to light activities within two to four weeks and full work within six to eight weeks. After lumbar decompression for stenosis, rehabilitation takes three to six months for maximum benefit. Chronic back pain recovery is measured in improvements in function and quality of life over months to years rather than complete resolution.
complications: Chronic back pain — transition from acute to chronic pain in approximately 10-15% of presentations, associated with significant disability, depression, and reduced quality of life.

Cauda equina syndrome — rare but serious compression of the terminal spinal nerve roots causing bladder and bowel dysfunction and saddle anaesthesia; a surgical emergency with permanent deficits if delayed.
Progressive neurological deficit — worsening weakness or numbness in a limb from ongoing nerve root or spinal cord compression, which may become permanent without decompression.
Failed back surgery syndrome — persistent or recurrent pain after one or more spinal operations, occurring in approximately 10-40% depending on patient selection and surgical indication.
Depression and reduced quality of life — strongly co-occurring with chronic back pain in a bidirectional relationship; untreated psychological comorbidity significantly worsens pain and function.
Disability and occupational loss — chronic back pain is the leading cause of years lived with disability worldwide, with substantial personal, social, and economic consequences.

supportResources.name: NICE — Low Back Pain and Sciatica in Over 16s: Assessment and Management
supportResources.description: UK clinical guideline providing evidence-based recommendations for assessment and management of low back pain and sciatica in adults.

supportResources.name: NHS — Back Pain
supportResources.description: Plain-English NHS patient information on causes, self-care, and when to seek help for back pain.

supportResources.name: American College of Physicians — Patient Information on Low Back Pain
supportResources.description: Summary of ACP guidelines for patients covering treatments for acute and chronic low back pain.

sources.title: Hartvigsen J et al. What low back pain is and why we need to pay attention. Lancet 2018
Qaseem A et al. Noninvasive Treatments for Acute, Subacute, and Chronic Low Back Pain: ACP Clinical Practice Guideline. Ann Intern Med 2017
NICE NG59 — Low Back Pain and Sciatica in Over 16s: Assessment and Management (2016)
Maher C et al. Non-specific low back pain. Lancet 2017
Vos T et al. Global Burden of Disease 2020 — low back pain estimates. Lancet Rheumatol 2023
Deyo RA et al. Overtreating Chronic Back Pain: Time to Back Off? JAMA 2009
WHO — Musculoskeletal health: back pain fact sheet

confusedWithConditions.name: Kidney stones
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Renal colic from kidney stones causes acute, severe flank pain that radiates to the groin, is unrelated to spinal movement, and is accompanied by haematuria; true back pain arises from spinal structures and is position-dependent.

confusedWithConditions.name: Ankylosing spondylitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Ankylosing spondylitis causes inflammatory back pain in young adults: morning stiffness over 30 minutes that improves with exercise, elevated inflammatory markers, and sacroiliac joint changes on MRI — all absent in non-specific mechanical dorsalgia.

confusedWithConditions.name: Fibromyalgia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Fibromyalgia causes widespread musculoskeletal pain with fatigue, sleep disturbance, and multiple tender points across the body; back pain in fibromyalgia is diffuse and accompanied by other systemic symptoms, unlike focal mechanical dorsalgia.

confusedWithConditions.name: Sciatica
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Sciatica denotes radicular leg pain from sciatic nerve root compression, which travels below the knee; it can coexist with back pain but the defining feature is the leg radiation rather than back pain alone.

relatedConditions.name: Sciatica
relatedConditions.relevance: Sciatica is the most common neurological complication of lumbar disc herniation causing dorsalgia; distinguishing the two guides imaging, treatment, and surgical decision-making.

relatedConditions.name: Herniated disc
relatedConditions.relevance: Disc herniation is the most common specific structural cause of acute lumbar dorsalgia with radiculopathy.

relatedConditions.name: Lumbar radiculopathy
relatedConditions.relevance: Nerve root compression at lumbar levels produces both back pain and characteristic leg symptoms; radiculopathy changes the diagnostic and treatment approach compared to axial pain alone.

relatedConditions.name: Spinal stenosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Lumbar stenosis causes dorsalgia with neurogenic claudication in older adults and is the most common indication for spine surgery in people over 65.

faqs.question: What is dorsalgia?
faqs.answer: Dorsalgia is the medical term for back pain affecting any part of the spine — neck, mid-back, or lower back. When no specific cause has been identified, the ICD-10 code M54.9 is used. It is the most common cause of disability worldwide.

faqs.question: What is the most common cause of back pain?
faqs.answer: The most common cause is non-specific mechanical low back pain — pain from muscles, ligaments, discs, or facet joints without a single identifiable structural cause. It accounts for approximately 90% of back pain presentations and usually resolves within 4-6 weeks.

faqs.question: When should I worry about back pain?
faqs.answer: Seek urgent medical attention for saddle numbness or loss of bladder or bowel control, rapid leg weakness, back pain with fever or weight loss, or severe pain after trauma. These red flag features require same-day assessment to exclude serious causes.

faqs.question: Is bed rest good for back pain?
faqs.answer: No. Bed rest for more than 1-2 days is associated with worse outcomes for most back pain. Staying gently active — walking, stretching, and continuing light daily tasks — helps recovery more than resting. Structured exercise is the most evidence-based treatment.

faqs.question: What is the best treatment for back pain?
faqs.answer: For most acute back pain, staying active is the most effective intervention. NSAIDs like ibuprofen provide short-term relief. Physiotherapy and supervised exercise are the strongest long-term treatments. Surgery is reserved for specific structural causes with neurological involvement that fail conservative management.

faqs.question: Does back pain show up on X-ray?
faqs.answer: X-rays show bones — fractures, alignment, and disc space narrowing — but cannot image discs, nerves, or soft tissues. Most non-specific back pain is not visible on X-ray. MRI is the preferred test when imaging is needed because it shows disc, nerve, and tissue detail.

faqs.question: How long does back pain usually last?
faqs.answer: Most acute episodes of mechanical back pain improve substantially within 2-4 weeks and resolve by 6-8 weeks. About 10-15% of cases become chronic (lasting more than 12 weeks). Radicular pain from disc herniation typically improves over 6-12 weeks.

faqs.question: Can back pain be caused by stress?
faqs.answer: Yes. Psychological factors including stress, anxiety, depression, and fear of movement are among the strongest predictors of chronic back pain. They amplify pain perception through central sensitisation and reduce the likelihood of recovery. Addressing these factors is a core part of treatment.

faqs.question: What is cauda equina syndrome?
faqs.answer: Cauda equina syndrome occurs when a large disc herniation or other lesion compresses all the nerve roots at the base of the spine simultaneously, causing saddle anaesthesia, loss of bladder or bowel control, and leg weakness. It is a surgical emergency — delays beyond hours risk permanent incontinence.

faqs.question: Is exercise safe for back pain?
faqs.answer: Yes, for most types of back pain. Staying active and engaging in gradually progressive exercise is one of the most effective treatments. Supervised physiotherapy programmes reduce pain and prevent recurrence. Avoid heavy axial loading or high-impact exercise during an acute episode until assessed.

faqs.question: What is sciatica and how is it different from back pain?
faqs.answer: Sciatica is leg pain caused by compression of the sciatic nerve root in the lumbar spine, producing pain, numbness, or weakness from the back down through the buttock and leg below the knee. Back pain describes pain localised to the spine itself. The two often occur together but have different implications for treatment.

faqs.question: When is back surgery needed?
faqs.answer: Surgery is rarely the first answer. It is considered for disc herniation when radiculopathy persists beyond 6-12 weeks despite conservative treatment or when there is progressive neurological deficit. Spinal stenosis with severe walking limitation unresponsive to conservative care may require decompressive surgery.

faqs.question: Can losing weight help back pain?
faqs.answer: Yes. Excess body weight increases compressive loading on lumbar discs and facet joints. Weight loss through diet and exercise reliably reduces back pain severity and improves function in people who are overweight or obese, and also reduces the risk of disc herniation and nerve compression.

faqs.question: What is spinal stenosis?
faqs.answer: Spinal stenosis is narrowing of the spinal canal that compresses the nerve roots or spinal cord, typically through a combination of disc bulging, facet joint enlargement, and ligamentum flavum thickening with age. It causes pain and leg weakness that worsens with walking and is relieved by sitting or bending forward.

faqs.question: Does heat or ice help back pain?
faqs.answer: Heat works well for muscle spasm and chronic stiffness; a warm pack for 15-20 minutes reduces tightness and improves flexibility. Ice is more helpful in the first 48-72 hours after an acute injury to reduce inflammation. Neither is a long-term solution, but both can ease symptoms as part of self-management.

faqs.question: Can back pain cause leg weakness?
faqs.answer: Yes. When a herniated disc or spinal stenosis compresses a nerve root, it can impair nerve signals to the leg muscles, causing weakness in specific muscle groups — foot drop, difficulty rising on tiptoes, or quad weakness depending on the level. Progressive leg weakness requires urgent assessment.

faqs.question: Is back pain linked to depression?
faqs.answer: Yes, in both directions. Chronic back pain increases rates of depression and anxiety, and pre-existing depression makes back pain more likely to become chronic. Both should be treated simultaneously — treating only the pain without addressing mood, or vice versa, leads to worse outcomes.

faqs.question: What is a herniated disc?
faqs.answer: A herniated (slipped) disc occurs when the soft inner nucleus pulposus of an intervertebral disc pushes through a tear in the outer annulus fibrosus, pressing against a nearby nerve root. It is the most common specific structural cause of acute back pain with radiation into the arm or leg.

faqs.question: Are back pain injections effective?
faqs.answer: Epidural corticosteroid injections provide meaningful short-term pain relief in about 40-70% of patients with nerve root compression from disc herniation, helping them participate in rehabilitation. The effect typically lasts weeks to a few months and does not change the long-term outcome, so injections are used as a bridge to recovery rather than a cure.

faqs.question: How can I prevent back pain from recurring?
faqs.answer: The most effective prevention is regular exercise focusing on core and posterior chain strength, maintaining a healthy weight, using correct lifting technique, avoiding prolonged sitting without breaks, and quitting smoking. Addressing psychological factors such as stress and catastrophising also reduces recurrence risk.

metaTitle: Dorsalgia (Back Pain): Symptoms, Causes & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Dorsalgia (back pain) affects 619 million people globally. Learn causes, red flag signs, exercise therapy, and when surgery is needed. Doctor-reviewed guide.
